title: Third-Party Type Definitions
content:
Third-Party Type Definitions

TypeScript uses type definitions to understand the types of external JavaScript libraries.

### DefinitelyTyped

The [DefinitelyTyped](https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped) repository hosts community-maintained type definitions for thousands of libraries.

### Installing Type Definitions

```bash
# For libraries that ship with types (e.g., lodash has @types/lodash)
npm install --save-dev @types/lodash

# Some libraries include types (e.g., React 18+)
npm install react  # types are included
```

### Checking for Types

```bash
# Check if a library has built-in types
npm info react types

# Or check if @types package exists
npm view @types/react versions
```

### Using Typed Libraries

```typescript
import _ from "lodash"; // Full type support with @types/lodash

const numbers: number[] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const shuffled: number[] = _.shuffle(numbers);

import express from "express";
const app = express(); // Express has built-in types
```

### Libraries Without Types

When a library has no types, create an ambient declaration:

```typescript
// src/types/untyped-library.d.ts
declare module "untyped-library" {
  export function doSomething(): void;
  export const VERSION: string;
}
```

### skipLibCheck

In `tsconfig.json`, `skipLibCheck: true` skips type checking of `.d.ts` files for faster compilation:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "skipLibCheck": true
  }
}
```

> **Key Point:** Install `@types/<package>` for libraries that don't include their own type definitions.
